source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 9.1 Generalities (p. 157)

questions.q: What do PID and PPID identify?
options: PID is a process's unique identifier; PPID is the identifier of its parent process
PID is the parent's ID; PPID is the process's own ID
PID is the port ID; PPID is the protocol ID
PID is the priority; PPID is the parent priority
questions.why: Each process has a unique PID (Process IDentifier) and a PPID (Parent Process IDentifier).

questions.q: Which process is the ancestor of all other processes?
options: The init process (father of all processes by successive filiation)
The bash process
The kthreadd process
The root process
questions.why: By successive filiation, init is the father of all processes.

questions.q: How is a child process created, and what is returned to the parent?
options: The parent calls fork() to duplicate its code; the child's PID is returned to the parent
The child calls fork() on itself; nothing is returned
The kernel creates it with no parent involvement
The parent's PID is returned to the child only
questions.why: A child results from the parent calling fork() to duplicate its code; the child's PID goes back to the parent so it can communicate, and each child carries its parent's PID as its PPID.

questions.q: If you run the same command several times, what happens to the PID?
options: A different PID is assigned each time; a finished process's number becomes available again
The same PID is reused every time
The PID stays fixed per command name
No PID is assigned until the command finishes
questions.why: The PID represents the process at execution time; when it ends the number is freed, so repeated runs get different PIDs.

questions.q: What is the difference between a process and a thread?
options: Each process has its own memory context; threads of the same process share that context
Threads each have their own memory; processes share one
They are the same thing
A thread always has a lower PID than its process
questions.why: A process owns its resources and address space; threads within one process share that context.
